summary: DHH argues that the definition of 'normal' has narrowed dramatically since the 1980s and 90s, when kids with various quirks and differences were simply considered part of the normal spectrum. He contends that society now incentivizes identifying with marginalized categories, making it socially desirable to acquire diagnostic labels rather than be seen as ordinary. Referencing Abigail Shrier's 'Bad Therapy,' he points to a therapy industry eager to pathologize normal childhood behaviors. He calls for re-expanding the definition of normal to encompass a broader range of human variation, with fewer labels and fewer medical interventions.
key_points: Behaviors once considered normal childhood variation are now routinely pathologized with diagnostic labels
'Centering the margins' has made it socially desirable to identify as non-normal and low status to be regular
A therapy industry has grown eager to supply diagnoses and pills for what were once seen as normal phases of development
The pursuit of individuality through marginalized identity has replaced older forms of social differentiation like brand preferences
The definition of normal should be re-expanded to fit a broader spectrum, with labels reserved for severe cases
money_quotes: Nobody seems to qualify (or desire to be seen) as being normal these days.
This is the natural consequence of 'centering the margins'. Of making it socially desirable to be not normal and low status to be regular.
There's cachet of cool to be had in identifying with some margin. Preferably one that can claim to be oppressed by society via heteronormativity or neurotypicality or other big words for 'normal'.
There's a large industry of therapists and other 'mental health professionals' eager to accommodate this flight to the margins.
I think we've given up on something important in this pursuit of individuality through the spectrum of marginalization.
When fitting in is less about having the right brand of backpack and more about having some medical prescription.
We're better off re-expanding the definition of normal to fit a much broader spectrum of quirky, weird, and natural varieties of humans.
key_insight: Society has narrowed the definition of normal to the point where ordinary human variation is pathologized, and we should reverse course by expanding what counts as normal rather than labeling every difference.
